title: Moxham Family's Organic Milk Now Comes as Gelato
description_clean: Horowhenua organic dairy farmers Ange and Wayne Moxham have taken their business to a new level. They have just been named as Fonterra's top organic performer for milksolids and that coincides with them developing a new exciting product from their milk - namely gelato to complement their organic milk sales. The Moxhams say they are delighted and greatly encouraged to receive the award and say it's also a testament to their staff on their two farms in Koputaroa, a small rural community about 8km north of Levin. The larger of the two farms runs 500 cows on 240 hectares, and the smaller 70ha one
summary: Horowhenua organic dairy farmers Ange and Wayne Moxham have been named Fonterra's top organic performer for milksolids and have developed a new gelato product from their milk, produced across their two farms in Koputaroa.

title: Civil Circus!
description_clean: OPINION: It really is getting harder to lampoon government bureaucrats when they do such a good job of it themselves. Anyone in the primary sector will know about the clowns covering the countryside in the red tape we all have to wade through. Spare a thought then for media outfit Stuff who were threatened with fines up to $200,000 by a snivelling civil servant from the MoH for illustrating a quiz question with a photo of part of the cover of a 45-year-old copy Playboy. As Stuff notes, “It wasn’t the Playboy cover which caused offence. No, it was the back page which… featured an advert for a c
summary: An opinion piece criticises government bureaucracy, citing a case where a Ministry of Health official threatened media outlet Stuff with fines up to $200,000 over a photo of a 45-year-old Playboy cover used in a quiz.

title: Ewe Zealand First
description_clean: OPINION: After all the criticism that Winston Peters leveled at the India/NZ FTA, it seemed just a trifle ironic to the Hound that the first NZ politician to visit India after Prime Minister Modi's visit was a member of the Ewe Zealand First party. Mark Patterson, farmer and well-known champion of NZ wool, was right in going to India to promote our wool. The slight downside has been the new pun word it has created, such as - was this a ewe turn? Did Winston have the wool pulled over his eyes? Is Winston now feeling a bit sheepish and will this ewe stance be reflected in the polls? The big ques
summary: An opinion piece notes the irony of NZ First MP Mark Patterson, a farmer and wool champion, being the first NZ politician to visit India to promote wool after Winston Peters criticised the India/NZ FTA.

title: Faulkner: Take Rural Infrastructure Out of Politics
description_clean: Federated Farmers' new vice president Sandra Faulkner says the issue of adverse events emergency management and infrastructure needs to be taken out of the three-year political cycle. She says there needs to be cross party support that stretches out over thirty years to get an idea of the spend and bring some certainty. "Hopefully the emergency management legislation which is now before parliament will not suffer from political interference," she told Rural News . "Those rural people who live at the end of the shingle road, across a bridge or up in the ranges need to feel connected to our soci
summary: Federated Farmers' new vice president Sandra Faulkner says adverse events emergency management and rural infrastructure should be taken out of the three-year political cycle, calling for cross-party support over a 30-year horizon.

title: B+LNZ Employment Hub: Farm Staff Support, Refreshed
description_clean: Building a strong team is one of the biggest drivers of a successful sheep and beef business, but it can also be one of the tougher parts to get right. That's according to Olivia Weatherburn, B+LNZ's national extension programme manager. "Farmers today are wearing a lot of hats. Alongside running the farm, they're also managing people, systems and more. "We heard clearly that farmers want more support to get the most from their people, and that's where the Employment Hub comes in. "It pulls together practical, easy-to-use resources to help farmers through each step, whether it's hiring someone
summary: Beef + Lamb New Zealand has refreshed its Employment Hub, offering practical resources to help sheep and beef farmers with hiring and managing staff, according to national extension programme manager Olivia Weatherburn.

title: NZ Blueberry Industry Sounds Alarm Over MPI Import Plan
description_clean: Blueberry growers are warning that fresh imports could pose a huge biosecurity risk for NZ’s horticulture sector. Michaela Frost, Mamaku Blue Blueberry in Rotorua, says growers are baffled by Ministry for Primary Industries (MPI) plans to allow fresh blueberry imports from five countries - Mexico, Peru, Chile, US and Morocco. Consultation has recently closed for Biosecurity New Zealand’s proposed import requirements for fresh blueberries. But industry group Blueberries New Zealand says it’s worried about increased biosecurity risks - for example, bringing in the Spotted Wing Drosophila fruit f
summary: New Zealand blueberry growers are warning that MPI's plans to allow fresh blueberry imports from Mexico, Peru, Chile, the US and Morocco could pose a significant biosecurity risk, including pests such as Spotted Wing Drosophila.

title: 'Need to pedal hard': global competitors are pushing production, logistics

title: US Launches Lamb Safeguard Probe, NZ Exports at Risk
description_clean: As expected, the US has launched a safeguard investigation into lamb imports, a move that could impact New Zealand sheepmeat returns. The US Trade Representative has formally requested the International Trade Commission launch a safeguard investigation into US lamb imports. This investigation is not specifically directed at New Zealand and will be carried out by the US International Trade Commission, an independent non-partisan, quasi-judicial federal agency. The investigation is expected to take 4-6 months. New Zealand lamb earns an average of $21.99 per kilogram in the US, compared to New Ze
summary: The US has launched a safeguard investigation into lamb imports, a move that could affect New Zealand sheepmeat returns; the 4-6 month probe is not specifically directed at New Zealand, which earns an average of $21.99 per kilogram for lamb in the US.

title: Milk Glut Drags Fonterra Forecast Down to $9.20/kgMS
description_clean: A milk glut around the world shows no sign of easing, putting downward pressure on farmgate milk prices for the next few months. Since May, Global Dairy Trade (GDT) prices have fallen 11% across the reference products that inform Fonterra's farmgate milk price. This prompted the co-operative to lower its forecast farmgate milk price by 50c to $9.25/kgMS, with a new range of $8-$10.50/kgMS. ANZ agricultural economist Matt Dilly isn't surprised by Fonterra's revised milk price forecast at $9.25/kgMS. Dilly told Rural News that this change brings Fonterra's forecast into alignment with ANZ's fore
summary: Fonterra has lowered its forecast farmgate milk price by 50c to $9.25/kgMS with a range of $8-$10.50, as a global milk glut and an 11% fall in GDT prices since May put downward pressure on prices.

title: PC1 Pause Looms as Coalition Targets Waikato Farm Rules
description_clean: A pause on Waikato Regional Council's (WRC) plan change that sets new freshwater rules for farming could be announced in the coming weeks. All three parties in the coalition government want WRC's Plan Change 1 (PC1) paused until the Resource Management Act is replaced with two new laws: the Planning Bill and the Natural Environment Bill. A parliamentary select committee reports back on the Natural Environment Bill this week. Rural News understands a pause could be announced after the second reading of the bill in Parliament. Associate Agriculture Minister Andrew Hoggard says his party wants fe
summary: A pause on Waikato Regional Council's Plan Change 1 freshwater farming rules could be announced soon, as all three coalition government parties want it paused until the Resource Management Act is replaced by two new laws.
